coredata.dc:title: Fast and efficient “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor from N-doped carbon dots for detection of mercury and iodine ions in environmental water
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongli
Zhou, Penghui
Wu, Yalin
Su, Xiaoyan
Liu, Huanjia
Zhu, Guifen
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: A kind of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was facilely fabricated from polyethyleneimine and anhydrous citric acid, and which was adopted to develop a neoteric “on-off” and “off-on” fluorescent sensor for rapidly and efficiently sensing Hg2+ and I−. The fluorescence of N-CDs was notably quenched (off) in the existence of Hg2+ derived from strong interaction and the electron transfer between N-CDs and Hg2+, while the quenched fluorescence of the N-CDs and Hg2+ system was strikingly regained by addition of I− (on) resulted from the separation of N-CDs and Hg2+ due to the higher binding preference between Hg2+ and I−. Under optimal conditions, the linear detection ranges were 0.01–20 μM for Hg2+ and 0.025–7 μM for I−, respectively. Meanwhile, the detection limits could be down to 3.3 nM for Hg2+ and 8.5 nM for I−, respectively. Satisfied recoveries had also been gained for measuring Hg2+ and I− in practical water samples. The constructed “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor provided a simple, rapid, robust and reliable platform for detecting Hg2+ and I− in environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mercury ion
Iodine ion
“On-off-on” fluorescent sensor
N-CDs
Polyethyleneimine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection for Hg2+ and I−
Precisions and stability for sensing Hg2+ and I−

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22106039 , 21976211 ), Key Scientific Research Project Plan of Henan Province ( 20A610007 ), Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in University of Henan Province (No. 20IRTSTHN011 ) and Science and Technology Open Cooperation Project of Henan Academy of Sciences ( 210903019 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the N-CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of the N-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption (a) and fluorescence (excitation spectrum: b, emission spectrum: c) spectra of the N-CDs. (B) The contour plot (3D plot) of the N-CDs. (C) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths. (D) Effect of the pH values on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs. (E) Effect of the concentration of NaCl solution on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs. (F) The stability of the N-CDs.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of N-CDs under different conditions (N-CDs: 0.3 μg mL−1, Hg2+: 6 μM, I−: 6 μM). (B) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the N-CDs, N-CDs + Hg2+ and N-CDs + Hg2++I− (N-CDs: 0.3 μg mL−1, Hg2+: 6 μM, I−: 6 μM). (C) Fluorescence decay curves of the N-CDs, N-CDs + Hg2+ and N-CDs + Hg2++I− (N-CDs: 0.3 μg mL−1, Hg2+: 6 μM, I−: 6 μM). (D) Zeta potentials of the N-CDs, N-CDs + Hg2+ and N-CDs + Hg2++I− under the same condition (N-CDs: 0.3 μg mL−1, Hg2+: 6 μM, I−: 6 μM).
(A)The contour plot of N-CDs + Hg2+. (B) The optimization of the N-CDs concentration. (C) The optimization of the pH value. (D) The optimization of the incubation time.

(A) The fluorescence responses of the N-CDs in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations. (B) A linear correlation of F0/F-1 values against the Hg2+ concentration within the range of 0.010–20 μM (Insert: the plot of the F0/F-1 values versus the Hg2+ concentration over the range from 0 μM to 21 μM).
Selectivity of the N-CDs for Hg2+ over other metal ions (single) and the mixture of Hg2+ with other metal ions (mixture).
(A)The contour plot of N-CDs + Hg2++I−. (B) The optimization of the N-CDs concentration. (C) The optimization of the pH value. (D) The optimization of the incubation time.

(A) The fluorescence responses of the N-CDs + Hg2+ system with the presence of different I− concentrations. (B) A linear correlation of ΔF values against the I− concentration within the range of 0.025–7 μM (Insert: the plot of the ΔF values versus the Hg2+ concentration over the range from 0 μM to 8 μM).
Selectivity of the N-CDs + Hg2+ system for I− over other anion ions (single) and the mixture of I− with other anion ions (mixture).
The stability of this method for sensing Hg2+ and I−.
Illustration of the synthesis of the fluorescent sensor and the working principle for the detection of Hg2+ and I−.
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of Hg2+.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: B-MoS2 QDs: boronic acid functionalized MoS2 quantum dots.
AuNCs-DAP system: gold nanoclusters and 2,3-diaminophenazine system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of I−.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: TPE-QN: tetraphenylethene (TPE)-functionalized quinolinium salts with hexafluorophosphate.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked recoveries for Hg2+ and I− in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Fast and efficient “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor from N-doped carbon dots for detection of mercury and iodine ions in environmental water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control
Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, Henan Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Control, Key Laboratory for Yellow River and Huai River Water Environmental and Pollution Control, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, Henan 453007, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Eco-Environmental Protection, Beijing 10037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Eco-Environmental Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Municipal Research Institute of Eco-Environmental Protection, Beijing 10037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A kind of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was facilely fabricated from polyethyleneimine and anhydrous citric acid, and which was adopted to develop a neoteric “on-off” and “off-on” fluorescent sensor for rapidly and efficiently sensing Hg2+ and I−. The fluorescence of N-CDs was notably quenched (off) in the existence of Hg2+ derived from strong interaction and the electron transfer between N-CDs and Hg2+, while the quenched fluorescence of the N-CDs and Hg2+ system was strikingly regained by addition of I− (on) resulted from the separation of N-CDs and Hg2+ due to the higher binding preference between Hg2+ and I−. Under optimal conditions, the linear detection ranges were 0.01–20 μM for Hg2+ and 0.025–7 μM for I−, respectively. Meanwhile, the detection limits could be down to 3.3 nM for Hg2+ and 8.5 nM for I−, respectively. Satisfied recoveries had also been gained for measuring Hg2+ and I− in practical water samples. The constructed “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor provided a simple, rapid, robust and reliable platform for detecting Hg2+ and I− in environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As a kind of fatal metal ions, mercury ion (Hg2+) exists universally in various environmental media in many regions over the world, especially in water. Hg2+ can lead to environmental pollution and cause kidney disease, immune system disease and other sicknesses to animals and humans even at trace level due to its bioaccumulation and direct toxicity (Aragay et al., 2011; Bera et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2015; Yuen et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2021). Iodine is an important trace element and biological anion and plays critical role in scores of biological processes which contain thyroid function and neurological activity. What's more, the concentration of iodine is directly concerned with normal human growth and development. Iodine exists mostly in the form of iodide (I−). Iodine deficiency and excess could both result in some different and serious problems such as poor thyroid function or thyroid disorders (Gavrilenko et al., 2019; Ho and Leclerc, 2003; Niaz et al., 2018; Pena-Pereira et al., 2019). Therefore, it is of great significance to detect Hg2+ and I− in environmental samples with high sensitivity. However, the traditional detection methods exist some considerable disadvantages such as high cost, complicate operations and consuming time (Liu et al., 2018; Shelor and Dasgupta, 2011; Tabaraki and Sadeghinejad, 2018). In consequence, it is imperative and urgent to explore some rapid, economic and sensitive strategies for determination of Hg2+ and I−.

Recently, fluorescence analysis methods have gained increase concerns due to their low cost, technical simplicity and high selectivity and sensitivity (Carter et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2021a, 2021b; Sidhu et al., 2019). Fluorescent materials utilized as fluorescent sensors are mainly organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, noble metal-based nanocluster and carbon dots (CDs) (Chen et al., 2021; Han et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021), etc. However, there are also some shortcomings in some materials containing emission spectrum width and asymmetry, small stokes shift, strong toxicity and high cost. Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a novel kind of nanomaterials with carbon as the framework and rich in functional groups, has emerged like a rising star in fluorescence sensing and other fields. CDs are generally spherical or subspherical, with good water solubility, strong fluorescence stability and excellent biological compatibility, which endow it superior advantages of low toxicity, distinguished photostability, easy preparation and wide carbon sources (Liu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021b; Sun et al., 2021; Xiao and Sun, 2018). The fluorescent sensors derived from CDs possess the characteristics of simplicity, speediness, competitive price, good stability and high selectivity (Kailasa and Koduru, 2022; Lin et al., 2018). To date, many CDs have been synthesized with various carbon sources. Yang et al. (Yang et al., 2018) fabricated phosphorus-doped CDs (P-CDs) with sodium citrate and phytic acid. The obtained P-CDs could be served as the fluorescent sensor for sensitively sensing Cu2+ resulted from the formation of P-CDs-Cu2+ chelate. This method possessed the low detection limit of 1 nM for sensing Cu2+ in water samples. Li et al. (Li et al., 2018) synthesized N, S co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) through a simple microwave-assisted route derived from L-cysteine and ammonium citrate. The fluorescence of N, S-CDs was dramatically decreased by levofloxacin which was put down to the electron transfer process from N, S-CDs to levofloxacin. This sensor had a wider linear range and the detection limit was 5.1 ng mL−1, and it was not hard to see that the designed sensor based on CDs owned the merits of simplicity, considerable flexibility and high sensitivity.

Inspired by the abovementioned achievements, a novel kind of fluorescent N-CDs was successfully fabricated using anhydrous citric acid as the carbon source, and polyethyleneimine as the dopant via a facile hydrothermal strategy. The obtained N-CDs possessed hydroxyl and amino groups on their surface derived from the characterization results, which offered the possibility for the interaction between N-CDs and target. As expected, the experiments results revealed that the prepared N-CDs could be served as an “on-off” fluorescent sensor to determine Hg2+, which was based on the electron transfer between CDs and Hg2+. In view of the strong combination effect between the Hg2+ and I−, the system of N-CDs + Hg2+ was innovatively utilized as an “off-on” fluorescent sensor for the detection of I−. The preparation of this sensor and its working principle for sensing Hg2+ and I− were illustrated in Scheme 1 . It was obvious that the fluorescence of the sensor was memorably quenched by Hg2+ due to the electron transfer as named “turn-off” effect. Additionally, the fluorescence of the sensor was then wonderfully recovered by I− resulting from the forcefully binding interaction between Hg2+ and I− as called “turn-on” effect. The developed “off-on” fluorescence sensor based on N-CDs was eco-friendly, simple and stable. Meanwhile, it realized the highly and rapidly selective and sensitive determination of two targets containing Hg2+ and I−. The designed N-CDs sensor provide a reliable and promising method for highly effect detection of Hg2+ and I− in environmental fields.

Polyethyleneimine (MW = 10,000 Da), anhydrous citric acid and quinine sulfate (98%) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other analytical-grade chemicals were received from the Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water was employed throughout all experiments.

The N-CDs were fabricated with polyethyleneimine and anhydrous citric acid according to the hydrothermal approach. Specifically, 0.6 g polyethyleneimine was dissolved in 10 mL purified water and mixed with 1.2 g anhydrous citric acid and another 10 mL purified water. Subsequently, the transparent solution was added into the Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL). The reaction was completed after heating at 200 °C for 4 h. The resulting solution was purified by acetone in a centrifuge (11,000 rpm, 10 min) and the gained precipitate was further dried in an oven. The gained N-CDs power was then re-dissolved in water and preserved at 4 °C with the concentration of 10 μg mL−1 for follow-up experiments.

A JEM-2100 TEM was adopted for characterizing the morphologies of the prepared N-CDs (JEOL, Akishimashi, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was achieved with KBr pellets by a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer (Thermo, New York, USA). A UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer was used for UV–Vis absorption analysis (SDPTOP, Shanghai, China). Fluorescence measurements were obtained on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Zeta potential measurements were performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZS zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Instrument, Malvern, England). Fluorescence lifetimes were performed on an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, Edinburgh, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection for Hg2+ and I−
ce:section.ce:para: In a representative detection route, N-CDs solution (30 μL, 10 μg mL−1) and certain amounts of Hg2+ solution (1, 10, 100 μM) were added in order and further diluted with purified water to 1.0 mL. The final concentration of Hg2+ could be obtained by calculation. The mixture was transferred to 1.0 mL quartz cell for quantitative determination. The fluorescence spectra were monitored when N-CDs were excited at 353 nm. The selectivity was performed on by adding other metal ions under the same conditions for sensing Hg2+.

In a typical detection process, N-CDs solution (30 μL, 10 μg mL−1) and Hg2+ solution (60 μL, 0.1 mM) were added to the centrifuge tube, and then different amounts of I− solution (1, 10, 100 μM) was also added. The samples were diluted in the same way and the final volumes were 1.0 mL. The measurements were performed under the same conditions as determination of Hg2+. The selectivity experiments of I− were further investigated via adding other anions into the solutions including F−, Cl−, Br−, S2−, SO4 2−, SO3 2− and so on instead of I−.

Measurement of Hg2+ and I− from four environmental water samples were used to evaluate the reliability and applicability of the “off-on” fluorescent sensor. The water samples were obtained from Changping Park, Binhe Park, Chaobai River, Jingmi Canal and pretreated with 0.22 μm microporous membranes for removing the insoluble substances. The analytical process was the same as the above-mentioned except that the purified water was replaced by the target environmental water sample. The samples were all measured with this sensor at blank and spiked at three levels of 0.05, 0.1 and 0.25 μM of Hg2+ and I−, respectively.

The TEM measurement (see Fig. 1A) provided the morphology of the synthesized N-CDs. It was evident that the N-CDs displayed nearly spherical shapes with uniform sizes, and possessed the mean size of 6.1 nm by randomly choosing and analyzing the sizes of 120 nanoparticles from the TEM images through the Nano Measurer 1.2 software (Fig. 1A). The surface manner of N-CDs was investigated through FTIR spectroscopy. As revealed in Fig. 1B, the strong characteristic peak at 3419 cm−1 was due to NH/OH stretching vibration. The two peaks located at 2936 cm−1 and 2860 cm−1 were resulted from the CH stretching vibration. The three peaks at 1654 cm−1, 1364 cm−1 and 1445 cm−1 were ascribed to the CO, CN stretching vibration and the OH bending vibration, respectively. While other two evident peaks of 1553 cm−1 and 752 cm−1 indicated the presence of NH bond (Kalytchuk et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). The water solubility of the obtained N-CDs was improved and the fluorescent properties were further enhanced by the above-mentioned hydrophilic groups on the surface of N-CDs (Deng et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2017).

Fig. 2A exhibits the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the gained N-CDs. There are two typical UV–Vis peaks. The peak at 241 nm is derived from the π-π* transition of the CC bond, while another obvious peak at 353 nm is ascribed to the n-π* transition of the -OH and NH2 (Wang et al., 2019b; Wang and Hu, 2014). These active groups not only offer excellent solubility but also enhance the fluorescence intensity which change the surface state of the N-CDs (Han et al., 2019; Song et al., 2017). As can be seen, the N-CDs had maximum emission at 435 nm with the excitation wavelength at 353 nm. Fig. 2B shows the 3D plot or contour plot between emission wavelength vs excitation wavelength and the related colors showed the different fluorescence intensities of N-CDs. It was apparent that the excitation wavelength of N-CDs was at 353 nm while the maximum emission was at 435 nm. Fig. 2C indicates that the prepared N-CDs own obvious excitation-independent multicolor emission feature, because the corresponding emission still keeps at 435 nm without distinct blue-shift or red-shift with the excitation wavelength varying from 290 nm to 390 nm. It might be closely derived from the mostly united dimension of the gained N-CDs and that was in accordance with the morphological observation (Wang et al., 2020). The N-CDs in aqueous solution showed a high quantum yield of 26.3% with quinine sulfate as the reference (QY 54%) (Li et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2017). Hence, the outstanding optical properties of N-CDs endows it to be an excellent fluorescent sensor.

The effect of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was demonstrated in Fig. 2D. The prepared N-CDs owned steady fluorescence over the pH range of pH 5–8 without significant change. However, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs changed to opposite direction over the pH ranges of pH 1–5 and pH 9–14. This phenomenon was probably ascribed to the surface groups of N-CDs. Fig. 2E shows the fluorescence stability of N-CDs at diverse ionic strength. It was notable that the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was kept stable without imperceptible change in the NaCl concentration range of 0–4.5 M, indicating that the N-CDs were of good resistance and photostability even at high concentrations of ionic strength. Above all, the obtained N-CDs owned an excellent application prospect as a fluorescent sensor under physiological conditions (Shi et al., 2015). In addition, it is obvious that the fluorescence intensities kept constant and were rather stable for two months when the CDs solution was preserved at 4 °C (Fig. 2F).

As exhibited in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 435 nm was significantly quenched in the existence of Hg2+ and which was later notably recovered by I−. This phenomenon demonstrated the prepared N-CDs could be utilized as a novel “off-on” fluorescent sensor for efficient determination of Hg2+ and I−. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs exhibited no noticeable variation without and with I− which indicated that N-CDs would not have the direct interaction with I−. UV–Vis spectra of the sensing platform were explored to verify the sensing mechanism. From Fig. 3B, no significant changes were found by comparing with the UV–Vis spectra of N-CDs, N-CDs + Hg2+ and N-CDs + Hg2++I−. There was no other different peak in the sensing system, which excluded the possibility of the static quenching. The static quenching was a kind of common fluorescent quenching mechanisms which made the absorption spectra change resulted from the appearance of the ground-state complex. The results suggested this quenching effect might be derived from the effective electron transfer between N-CDs and Hg2+ while the recovered fluorescence of N-CDs was probably put down to the higher combination effect between the Hg2+ and I−, which effectively blocked the electron transfer process and further made the fluorescence restore (Ding et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2017; Wei et al., 2020). Fluorescence lifetime measurements further offered support to our proposed mechanism. As expected, the lifetimes were various in the sensing system. The average lifetimes of N-CDs, N-CDs + Hg2+ and N-CDs + Hg2++I− were measured to be 11.33 ns, 10.06 ns and 10.36 ns (Fig. 3C), respectively. It was obvious that the lifetime firstly decreased and then increased with the addition of Hg2+ and I−, which further suggested the quenching might be a dynamic quenching process. The Zeta potentials were also measured to authenticate the proposed mechanism. Form Fig. 3D, the zeta-potential value of N-CDs was −1.04 mV, nevertheless, the value turned into +3.23 mV and + 0.915 mV with Hg2+ and I−, indicating that Hg2+ might be bonded on the surface of N-CDs and that was probably weakened by I− owing to the strong affinity between I− and Hg2+ (Zhang et al., 2019; Zu et al., 2017). Above all, the mechanism for sensing Hg2+ was effective electron transfer from N-CDs to Hg2+ and that was a dynamic quenching process. The intense reaction of Hg2+ and I− resulted in release of Hg2+ from N-CDs, which caused the fluorescence restoration of N-CDs. Thus, the gained N-CDs were of great potential for effectively detecting Hg2+ and I− based on the “off-on” principle.

It was obvious from Fig. 4A that Hg2+ possessed a distinct quenching effect on the fluorescence of N-CDs. The critical experimental parameters containing the concentration of N-CDs, pH value and the incubation time were optimized to gain better detection results. The F0/F-1 value (F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs without and with Hg2+, respectively.) was served as the signal for sensing Hg2+ in the optimization process.

Generally, the better detection performance and higher sensitivity could be gained at the lower N-CDs concentration whereas the broader linear range might come from a higher concentration, so the suitable N-CDs concentration was urgently needed in the detection process. Fig. 4B exhibits that the determination of Hg2+ was deeply affected by the N-CDs concentration, and the F0/F-1 value firstly rose due to the more CDs reacted with Hg2+ and later decreased with N-CDs concentration continuous increase which was ascribed to the more CDs need to react with more Hg2+ whereas the amount of Hg2+ was fixed in the optimization procedure. The highest value of F0/F-1 was achieved when the concentration of N-CDs was 0.30 μg mL−1. Thus, 0.30 μg mL−1 was applied as the proper concentration to sense Hg2+.

The influence of the pH was illustrated in Fig. 4C. The F0/F-1 value enhanced remarkably with pH varying from pH 5.0 to pH 7.0 and later diminished with further increase of pH value. There might be some reactions between some hydrogen ions and the amino groups on the surface of N-CDs at more acidic conditions, causing the decrease of the interaction between Hg2+ and N-CDs and further made the quenching effect weaken. Under strong alkali environment, Hg2+ is easily hydrolyzed and may bind with hydroxide ions, which further lessen the reaction between Hg2+ and N-CDs and receded the quenching effect (Fu et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019a). The results suggested that pH 7.0 was the suitable pH for subsequent experiments.

The incubation time was also an important factor for detecting Hg2+. As revealed in Fig. 4F, the F0/F-1 value gradually augmented when the time went on and almost remained unchanged within 1 min. Furthermore, it kept constant with the increase of the incubation time, which indicated that N-CDs could realize the fast and stable detection of Hg2+. Hence, the optimal incubation time was set at 2 min for gaining the reliable signal.

With the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased progressively in the optimal reaction conditions (Fig. 5A). It was apparent that the F0/F-1 value kept unchanged when the Hg2+ concentration was 21 μM (Fig. 5B). The F0/F-1 value was good linear response to Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.01–20 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ was 3.3 nM (based on 3σ/s), which was comparable with or superior to that of most previous reports (Babaee et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; Manivannan et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). The linear ranges and sensitivity of various fluorescent sensors for Hg2+ were listed in Table 1 . The results demonstrated the as-prepared N-CDs in present study achieved highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ and possessed certain practical significance.

The complicacy of environmental samples triggers the challenge for Hg2+ determination not merely in sensitivity but also in selectivity. Consequently, 20 diverse metal ions containing Fe3+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and so on were selected to value their impact on sensing Hg2+. As exhibited in Fig. 6 , these potentially interfering cations resulted in insignificant impact on the fluorescent detection for Hg2+, when they were added at 2 μM without and with Hg2+ at 0.05 μM in the system, which indicated that Hg2+ had a faster and closer affinity with N-CDs compared to other interfering cations. These results powerfully stated the developed fluorescent sensor possessed excellent selectivity.

The strong affinity between Hg2+ and I− provided the foundation to construct a novel “off-on” fluorescent sensor for quantitative determination of I−. As exhibited in Fig. 7A, the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs and Hg2+ system was expectedly and dramatically recovered with addition of I−. As before, the concentration of N-CDs, the pH value and the incubation time were also investigated to make the sensing performance better. In optimization steps, the ΔF value was used as the signal (ΔF was the difference of the fluorescence intensities with and without I−).

The influence of N-CDs concentration was illustrated in Fig. 7B, the ΔF value raised as N-CDs concentration increased and achieved the highest value at 0.30 μg mL−1 while decreased as N-CDs concentration further increased, which indicated that N-CDs concentration had a vital function for detecting I− and 0.30 μg mL−1 was the optimal N-CDs concentration for the follow-up detection of I−.

Fig. 7C exhibits the effect of pH on the fluorescent intensity of the detection system, and it was found that the ΔF value rose steadily to a peak value when the pH value of solution was up to pH 7.0 and later declined notably. The trend was the same as that of Hg2+ detection. The quenching experiments by Hg2+ owned the highest value at pH 7.0, suggesting the frailer quenching response would bring about the decrement of the ΔF value while the pH of the detection system was below or above pH 7.0. Therefore, pH 7.0 was optioned for sensing I−.

Fig. 7D displays that the fluorescence restoration of N-CDs + Hg2+ system on account of I− was prompt and steady with the continuous increase of time, suggesting the N-CDs + Hg2+ system could be employed for fast and simple sensing I−. Measurements were started immediately after the addition of I−.

It was obvious that the fluorescence of the N-CDs + Hg2+ system at 435 nm was gradually recovered when the concentration of I− was added in the range of 0–8 μM (Fig. 8A). The results exhibited that N-CDs turned from “off” mode by quenching of Hg2+ to “on” mode owing to the presence of I− which further demonstrated that the N-CDs + Hg2+ system was comparatively sensitive for detecting I−. It could be seen that the ΔF value had a fine linear correlation with the concentration of I− ranging from 0.025 μM to 7 μM (Fig. 8B). The LOD for I− was reckoned to be 8.5 nM on the basis of 3σ/s. The comparison between our constructed fluorescent sensor based on N-CDs + Hg2+ system and other reported fluorescent sensors for I− determination had been displayed in Table 2 . Although the detection range of our fluorescent sensor was not the widest in Table 2, the sensitivity of the proposed sensor was completely comparable or faintly better than some reported sensors (Hao et al., 2020; He et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2016; Zor et al., 2018), which demonstrated the “off-on” fluorescent sensor was most suitable for efficiently sensing I−.

To further confirm the selectivity of the “off-on” fluorescent sensor for sensing I−, plenty of experiments were carried out for measuring the fluorescence responses of the N-CDs + Hg2+ system with addition some common anions under the same analytical conditions. As shown in Fig. 9 (single), the fluorescence intensity of the detection system was recovered only with the existence of I− and did not considerably change with other anions. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensities were measured when the “off-on” fluorescent sensor was added simultaneously with I− and various competitive anions. As displayed in Fig. 9 (mixture), insignificant changes were obtained, which suggested the presence of the coexisting anions possessed inconspicuous effect on the fluorescence restoration. Hence, the above results substantially demonstrated the proposed “off-on” fluorescent sensor had outstanding specificity toward I− detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precisions and stability for sensing Hg2+ and I−
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the reliableness and steadiness of our presented strategy, the intra-day, inter-day precisions and stability experiments were also carried out. As the intra-day precision was involved, the fluorescence behavior of the “off-on” fluorescent sensor to both Hg2+ and I− concentration at three levels of 0.05, 0.10, 0.25 μM and were measured for six times a day. In the inter-day precision experiments, the fluorescence spectra to the same three concentrations of Hg2+ and I− were measured once a day and lasted six days. The consequences of these experiments exhibited the intra-day precisions of Hg2+ and I− for three levels were 2.3%, 1.1%, 1.3% and 3.3%, 2.7%, 1.7% (RSD, n = 6), respectively. The inter-day precisions of Hg2+ and I− were 3.4%, 2.9%, 1.8% and 3.8%, 3.0%, 2.2% (RSD, n = 6), respectively. In addition, the fluorescence responses of this “on-off” and “off-on” fluorescent sensor to the same concentrations of Hg2+ and I− at 0.25 μM were measured and persisted for 24 h. It was obvious that negligible changes on the values of F0/F-1 and ΔF were found within 24 h (Fig. 10 ). The experimental results suggested this novel fluorescent sensor owned outstanding stability, which validated the reproducibility of Hg2+ and I− detection.

Four environmental water samples were applied to verify the viability of the fluorescent sensor for sensing Hg2+ and I−. As listed in Table 3 , Hg2+ and I− were not found in real samples based on the proposed strategy. After spiked with Hg2+ and I−, the recoveries of Hg2+ ranged from 95.2% to 103.0% while that of I− were in the range from 96.8% to 101.0%, which were all within the acceptable ranges in the analysis, indicating that the fluorescent sensor was credible and accurate. Besides, the RSDs of Hg2+ and I− were satisfied. The experimental results exhibited the prepared fluorescent sensor could be a strong potential competitor for detecting Hg2+ and I− in environmental field.

A novel kind of N-CDs was successfully gained through a facile and simple hydrothermal treatment using polyethyleneimine and anhydrous citric acid as the precursors. Without further complicated aftertreatment, the obtained N-CDs could be directly served as a novel “on-off” and “off-on” fluorescent sensor for the efficient detection of Hg2+ and I− with excellent selectivity and sensitivity. The linear range for sensing Hg2+ was 0.01–20 μM with low LOD of 3.3 nM, and the linear range for detecting I− was 0.025–7 μM with a LOD of 8.5 nM. Furthermore, this fluorescent sensor was resoundingly employed for sensing Hg2+ and I− in practical samples with satisfied results and precisions. Therefore, the designed N-CDs sensor possessed great potentiality for economical and time-saving determination of Hg2+ and I− in environmental fields.

Yongli Liu: Project administration, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Penghui Zhou: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yalin Wu: Investigation. Xiaoyan Su: Writing - original draft. Huanjia Liu: Investigation, Software, Validation. Guifen Zhu: Investigation, Software, Validation. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22106039, 21976211), Key Scientific Research Project Plan of Henan Province (20A610007), Program for Innovative Research Team in Science and Technology in University of Henan Province (No. 20IRTSTHN011) and Science and Technology Open Cooperation Project of Henan Academy of Sciences (210903019).
